comments.text: GPT-5.4 Thinking finds the following relevant progress: 1. Let $G(p)$ denote the least prime primitive root modulo a prime $p$. Nongkynrih (Acta Arith. 72 (1995), 45–53) studied the distribution of prime primitive roots and proved that for almost all primes $p$ one has an asymptotic formula for the number of prime primitive roots $\le H$ is $N(H,p) \sim \frac{\varphi(p-1)}{p-1}\pi(H)$ provided $H$ is sufficiently large (polylogarithmic in $p$). Since $\varphi(p-1)/(p-1) \gg 1/\log\log p$, taking $H=(\log p)^A$ with $A$ large enough implies that $G(p) \le (\log p)^A$ for almost all primes $p$. In particular, for a set of primes of natural density $1$ there exists a prime primitive root $q \le (\log p)^A < p$. Thus the conjectured property holds for almost all primes $p$. 2. Related work of Martin (“The least prime primitive root and the shifted sieve”, 1997) shows that similar bounds for $G(p)$ follow from shifted sieve methods and that, assuming GRH, one obtains bounds implying $G(p) \ll (\log p)^C$ for all sufficiently large primes $p$. Under GRH this would therefore imply the statement of the problem for all sufficiently large $p$. 3. Computationally, extensive searches for the least prime primitive root $G(p)$ (e.g. Oliveira e Silva’s tables up to at least $10^{14}$) have not produced any counterexample to the statement that $G(p) < p$. Therefore, the problem appears to remain open in full generality, but it is known that $G(p) \le (\log p)^A$ for almost all primes $p$ (unconditionally), and that $G(p) \ll (\log p)^C$ for all sufficiently large primes $p$ under GRH. Both results imply that the conjecture holds for all sufficiently large primes in the corresponding sense. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Of course, $p > 2$ is required. Moreover, the (plausibly too) naive heuristic says this should be true (up to at most finitely many counterexamples): there are $\varphi(p-1) \ge \frac{cp}{\log \log p}$ many primitive roots modulo $p$, and $\frac{(1+o(1))p}{\log p}$ many primes below $p$. The probability that random sets with these growth rates don't intersect goes to 0 quite quickly.
